metadata.title: Grounding: The Only Reliable Way to Stop Claude from Hallucinating.

skin_warnings: B06: palette=claude but the outro is 'LogoOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Grounding: The Only Reliable Way to Stop Claude from Hallucinating.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Claude predicts the most probable answer, which is not the correct one, and a confident hallucination looks identical to a confident fact. Grounding has three parts: provide the source, answer only from it, and say I don't know when it is absent. Those three sentences drop the hallucination rate to near zero for questions the source can actually answer. Asking Claude to cite the exact passage creates an audit trail, because a non-verbatim citation is a detectable hallucination.

artifactLines: Claude predicts the most probable answer, which is not the correct one, and a confident hallucination looks identical to a confident fact.
Grounding has three parts: provide the source, answer only from it, and say I don't know when it is absent.
Those three sentences drop the hallucination rate to near zero for questions the source can actually answer.
Asking Claude to cite the exact passage creates an audit trail, because a non-verbatim citation is a detectable hallucination.

beats.narration_text: Here is why Grounding: The Only Reliable Way to Stop Claude from Hallucinating. belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

artifactLines: Claude predicts the most probable answer, which is not the correct one, and a confident hallucination looks identical to a confident fact.
Grounding has three parts: provide the source, answer only from it, and say I don't know when it is absent.
Those three sentences drop the hallucination rate to near zero for questions the source can actually answer.
Asking Claude to cite the exact passage creates an audit trail, because a non-verbatim citation is a detectable hallucination.

beats.narration_text: The point that confidence and correctness are uncorrelated for an ungrounded question changed how I read Claude's answers. I want to build the full grounding and citation workflow on one of my own documents. Can you help me (1) write the three-sentence grounding addition that ties answers to the source and permits 'I don't know', (2) add the instruction to cite the exact passage it draws from, and (3) design a quick verification step that flags any citation not appearing verbatim as a detectable hallucination?

props.segment: Grounding: The Only Reliable Way to Stop Claude from Hallucinating.
props.command: The point that confidence and correctness are uncorrelated for an ungrounded question changed how I read Claude's answers. I want to build the full grounding and citation workflow on one of my own documents. Can you help me (1) write the three-sentence grounding addition that ties answers to the source and permits 'I don't know', (2) add the instruction to cite the exact passage it draws from, and (3) design a quick verification step that flags any citation not appearing verbatim as a detectable hallucination?

beats.narration_text: Claude is a next-token predictor trained on vast text. When you ask a factual question without a source, it predicts the most probable answer given its training — which is not the same as the correct answer. Confidence and correctness are uncorrelated in this regime. A confident hallucination looks identical to a confident correct answer.

graphic.production_viz: Two-bar comparison: confidence vs correctness — uncorrelated. Confident wrong answer highlighted in terracotta.

beats.narration_text: Grounding has three parts. One: provide the source document in the prompt. Two: instruct Claude to answer only from that document. Three: instruct Claude to say 'I don't know' if the answer isn't in the source. The prompt addition is three sentences. The hallucination rate drops to near zero for questions answerable from the source.

graphic.production_viz: Three-step grounding recipe boxes. Before/after hallucination rate bar.

beats.narration_text: The next level: tell Claude to cite the exact passage it is drawing from. Then verify. A citation that doesn't appear verbatim in the source document is a hallucinated citation — which is a hallucination you can detect. The verifiable citation is the audit trail. If you can't find the passage in the source, the answer is not grounded.

graphic.production_viz: Citation workflow: answer -> cited passage -> source doc check. Hallucinated citation shows no match.

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude: 'Here is a document. Answer only from this text. If the answer isn't in the document, say you don't know. Cite the exact sentence you are drawing from.' Then paste any Wikipedia article and ask a question about it. What happens when you ask something the article doesn't cover? Run it. Kore, in for Humanitarians AI.

props.command: Here is a document. Answer only from this text. If the answer isn't in the document, say you don't know. Cite the exact sentence you are drawing from.
